is_p_falciparum.evidence: Study explicitly focuses on Plasmodium falciparum infections. Title states 'Plasmodium falciparum in northern Ethiopia' and abstract confirms '903 P. falciparum infections were sequenced.' All samples are microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum (Section 2.1: 'A total of 903 individuals with microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum infections were enrolled').

reports_target_markers.evidence: Study reports on kelch13 (k13) mutations including the validated R622I mutation (found in 44.3% of samples, 369/833) and C580Y mutation (0.4%, 2 samples). Also reports mutations in mdr1 (e.g., N86, Y184F, D1246), crt (CVIET haplotype, A220S, N326S, R371I), dhfr (N51I, C59R, S108N), and dhps (A437G, K540E, A581G). All are curated target markers from the specified list.

reports_extractable_frequencies.evidence: Numerator and denominator counts are provided throughout. For example: 'R622I mutation was found in 369/833 samples (44.3%)' with 95% CI. District-level breakdown: 'Gondar Zuria (51.5%, 95% CI 46.9-56.0%)' vs 'Tach Armachiho (35.4%, 95% CI 30.5-40.3%)' (369/486 and 382/486 for k13). Multiple supplementary tables (S1-S6) provide per-gene, per-district, per-Kebele, and per-month frequencies with n/N values.

sub_country_location.evidence: Study is based in two districts (Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho) in Amhara region, Ethiopia, encompassing 26 Kebeles (smallest administrative units). Data are broken down by district, by Kebele, and by specific health centers (Maksegnit and Sanja). Figures 1C and 1D and Tables S3 show Kebele-level prevalence estimates. Fig 1A maps individual Kebeles (red dots). All frequencies can be tied to sub-country locations.

temporal_resolution_within_3y.evidence: Study period is one year: 'November 01, 2022 to October 31, 2023' (Section 4.2). Data are reported by month throughout the study period (Section 2.7 and Figures 5A, S4-S6 Tables showing monthly prevalence). All reported frequencies fall within a ≤3-year temporal window; most are monthly or annual binning. Stated collection window is explicitly given: 'November 1, 2022–October 31, 2023.'

african_field_samples.evidence: Study enrolled 903 individuals with microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum infections from health facilities in two districts in Ethiopia's Amhara region, northwest Africa. Samples are from human field/clinical isolates collected in African health centers (Maksegnit and Sanja health centers in Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho districts). No mention of lab strains, imported cases, or non-African sites.

is_primary_study.evidence: This is a primary empirical study. It is a research article describing a health facility-based cross-sectional study with original data collection and sequencing: 'We conducted a health facility-based cross-sectional study over a year' (Section 4.3). Authors report original sequencing of 903 samples using MIP and long-read whole-genome sequencing. Not a review, meta-analysis, commentary, or pure modelling paper. Published in PLOS Pathogens as a research article.

duplicate_risk.evidence: No evidence of overlap with a larger consortium, named cohort, or surveillance network. The study is described as a novel year-long surveillance conducted by the authors in 2022-2023 in these two specific districts. While the authors cite previous studies in the same region (e.g., Fola et al. 2023, Brhane et al. 2025), those are separate studies. The data are available under new accession numbers (SAMN48791106–SAMN48792008) and BioProject PRJNA1269601, consistent with a new collection. Routine new field collections with no stated consortium membership carry low duplicate risk.

supplement.evidence: All necessary data for extraction are present in the main document and supplementary materials provided. Supplementary tables S1-S6 contain: S1 Table (overall prevalence of mutations by gene), S2 Table (prevalence by district), S3 Table (prevalence per Kebele), S4-S6 Tables (monthly prevalence overall and by district). S1 Data provides metadata and genotype file. Figures provide all key frequencies with confidence intervals and numerator/denominator information. No pointers to missing supplementary files are present; all referenced supplementary tables and figures are included or accessible.

assessment.summary: This is a primary empirical study of P. falciparum drug-resistance mutations in northwestern Ethiopia, based on 903 microscopically confirmed infections sampled over one year (November 2022–October 2023) from 26 Kebeles in two districts (Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho). The study uses MIP-targeted sequencing to detect WHO-validated and candidate artemisinin-resistance mutations in k13 and partner drug-resistance markers in mdr1, crt, dhfr, and dhps genes. Frequencies are reported with numerator/denominator counts and 95% confidence intervals at multiple geographic scales (overall, district, Kebele) and temporal scales (monthly, seasonal). All criterion requirements are met: P. falciparum confirmed, target markers reported (k13 R622I at 44.3%, k13 C580Y at 0.4%, plus partner-drug markers), extractable frequencies provided, sub-country resolution to Kebele level achieved, temporal resolution ≤3 years (monthly binning within a 1-year period), African field samples from Ethiopian health centers, and primary study design.
